speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is May the 30th, 1995. Survivor-- Ruth Kurland. Interviewer-- Elana Daniel. City/state, Chicago, Illinois. Country-- USA. Language-- English. You'll ask me before, and I'll answer you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May the 30th, 1995. My name is Elana Daniel, D-A-N-I-E-L. And I'm interviewing Ruth Kurland in Chicago, Illinois. Good afternoon. Please state your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ruth Kurland. K--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-U-R-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edgysz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-D-I-S-- S-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you repeat it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-D-G-I-S-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 11, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne. R-O-W-N-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell us about your family and childhood in Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was there with my father and mother. We were six children. I had four brothers and one sister. And we were living in a normal and a beautiful life. My oldest sister was born in 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sureh Zytrin. She was married, and she had a little girl, [INAUDIBLE]. She was seven years old. And then I had a brother, his name was Berel. He was born in 1919. And the Russian [INAUDIBLE] came in to us when they split up Poland. Half went to the German, and half went to the Russian. We were in the Russian side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me take you back to your childhood first. I want to hear more about who else lived in your household.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there, my-- my brother. He's 1924. And then one brother was eight. And the youngest one was six-year-old. And we lived all together in a very nice home. It was a religious home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And extended family? Would you tell us about it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a big family-- uncles and-- and aunties and cousins. It was hundreds of people. And I don't-- nobody's left over.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all lived in the same town with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some lived in the same town. Some lived in Kovel. My-- my sister was married-- to Kovel. And when it was a-- a wedding or something, everybody used to come together. And it was the happiest thing of the life. It was beautiful. Usually, when it was Saturday, we used to go to my grandfather, and there we used to celebrate Shabbos. And then it was, like, Pesach, there was a Seder that was long, and all the uncles and my cousins and the aunties used to come together. We used to-- to pray and sing and eat was-- until 3 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family Orthodox, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe the Jewish community at large? What type of institutions were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Rowne was a big city, and there was a lot of Jewish people, and there were Christian people. But the Jewish people have their own sections, usually they live together. And they-- there are organizations. There were [INAUDIBLE]. There were [? Shammara ?] [? Dati. ?] There were the B'nai B'rith. And every child coming from school, they used to belong to organizations. I belonged to [? Shammara ?] [? Dati. ?] This was a religious organization. And the thing was, we used to come there. We used to dance. We used to socialize. And we used-- always used to tell us to learn about Israel when-- and the history of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The synagogue, we used to go when I was younger. I used to go with my father, and my brothers, to the synagogue for Shabbos. And it was holidays we used to go there. And they always said-- they used to pray, and they used to-- it was a social thing. It was very nice. It was just beautiful. And come and gone by us Friday night, we made-- my father made-- kiddish. And-- and they used to sing Shalom Aleichem. And we ate very nice. My mother used to prepare. And they used to sing [? Miris, ?] and all the kids used to sing [? Miris. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that? Will you tell us what [? Miris is? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Miris ?] is in the prayer book, before you bench. And there is a prayer that you sing. And this was-- was just beautiful, everybody used to just sing. You eat then you sing. And-- and then we used to bench. And-- and we didn't have like radios and television. We used to read the papers. The older people used to read the papers. The younger kids used to read books. And it was a-- a wonderful, a wonderful place Friday night. It was so happy. And then neighbors used to come in and-- together. They used to make-- you know, they used to give something to-- to-- to drink, to eat, and it was-- it was something-- it was something beautiful. It was wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how large the Jewish community was?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was maybe about 60,000 Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how large was the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne was the-- was the main city, and then was little towns around the-- around Rowne. All kind of little towns. As a matter of fact, people come, say-- they all say that they are from Rowne. Rowne was a big city. There's a lot of stores, and-- and-- and there was beautiful shops. Everybody used to go for walks. There was a main street, and every shop was-- everybody used to go walking there. Used to get dressed up, and it was-- it was wonderful. But it's funny-- one side, Jewish people used to go walking. When they're outside, the-- the Polish people used to walk. But the-- you know, they-- they didn't-- some are anti-semitic. They-- if they could find my-- my brother when-- when he went around, and it came late, and a couple boys was running after him. They want to beat him up. So he was running in a bakery, and he-- and he hide himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any other Jewish institutions?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have, like, for all ages, institutions. I just remembered for the-- for the younger people, what there was. For the older-- for the older people, it was just for-- when you get older-- they used to-- Usually older people used to stay with their parents. They used to give-- didn't give away the-- the older people in institutions. There was one, but not too many.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we speak English and Russian, and in school, Polish. I speak Ukrainian, because there was my girlfriends. I got Ukrainian girlfriends, and I learned from them to speak Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish children were in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 30. But the teachers wasn't Jewish. They're all Polish teachers. Because to became a Jewish teacher was very hard when you went into university. So they used to beat up the Jewish students. So if a Jewish student-- if somebody wants to finish like the gymnasium, high school, and he wants to became something, he had to go to France or someplace else not important. Then they-- they get their education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there wasn't too many. Usually the girls, everybody was learning how to sew. Because there, if you want a dress, you couldn't go in a store and buy a dress like you go here. The used to be dressmakers. Used to sit and make dresses. I went to school in ORT, that I learned how to sew. This was after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- no, no, no. I finished school, and then I went to the [? ORT. ?] The [? ORT ?] was like high school, just that you make-- you learn a trade. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a contractor for painting. And he used to take a ladder, a-- usually from all the-- the-- the soldiers used [? to leave-- ?] all the places, he used to take. And he had a lot of people working. A lot of people were working for my father. Ukraine people, Polish people. And they used to say that my father is the best boss, and we are the children. And Friday morning, when they used to work in Rowne-- because they used to work all over Poland-- they used to come in every Friday. My mother used to bake pancakes, and make like cookies. 10 used to come in and finish eating, used to walk out and another 10 used to come and sit and eat. And then, for Christmas, my father used to buy them presents. And they used to come and say, Shymon, you're the best boss on everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and then, when Hitler came, my father was hiding because they start killing everybody. The same good friends went and they show Hitler where my father was. And they took and they killed him. When you were growing up in Rowne, did you experience any anti-semitism in school? Public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, the-- the teachers-- they were-- they used to-- my father was very good looking. He was tall and dark and handsome. She-- my teacher used to say, your father doesn't look Jewish. This was the only thing they said. He was Jewish. But otherwise, no. We were-- you know, it was a teacher and children. And-- the thing is that we were playing. So the other kids, if they can beat up a Jewish kid, they will beat him up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did experience anti-semitism outside of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe maybe some specific episodes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this one thing where my-- where my brother went and they was run after him, and want to beat him up. And one time where my uncle used to live, next door was Gentile people. And somebody came with a wagon, and accidentally the wagon went in the other side. And they came out with big sticks, and they beat up this man. And he was bleeding, and they had to take him to the hospital. So this was-- always during-- little things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were aware of it as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I saw the way they used to beat up these-- these people. Sure, we were afraid. We were afraid for the Christian people. Sometimes they used to go from church for Easter. So everybody was in the house. Because if they saw a Jew, they used to beat them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did people go out at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a Jewish neighborhood. See, the Jewish people lived in a different neighborhood. They used to go, because they used to go to the organizations. Everything was in-- in the Jewish neighborhood. We didn't go to the other neighborhoods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about Kristallnacht when it took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I didn't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know anything about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know about it. Did not about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the rise of Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing when they split up, you know, the Russians with them. They start bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is-- who-- who split up what?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they-- when they split up Poland. Remember? So-- so we were on the Russian side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, who split up what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler and Russia. They-- they made a pact and divided Poland. Half went to Germany, and half went to the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what side were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the Russian side, because we were near the Russian border. Rowne was near the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is this when-- when you found out about what is happening in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: They started bombing. They-- they-- they-- you know, the Germans came with the airplanes, and they were bombed. The-- this was in-- in '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, would you tell us, please, how your life changed under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they came in-- first of all, they cleaned out all the stores. There was beautiful stores with clothes, with everything. They came, and they took everything. They took it all from everything. My father couldn't work anymore, have people working for him, because he was, like, you know, from the rich people. So in other things they took away, you-- you couldn't leave like before.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your father couldn't work anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: He could work, but by himself. He couldn't have anybody with him. You know, he used to go to little houses and paint them by himself. They used to-- the soldiers and their wives used to come, and they used to go in the stores with beautiful things. Took everything. As a matter of fact, the-- the woman used to take her nightgowns and put it on for dresses. They thought it's dresses. They got-- they got a nightgown, and on top, they have four jackets. And they thought they were the best dresses-- dressed ladies there in town. Later, they find out that this is nightgowns, not a-- not a dress that you wear. So-- but the kids are-- they were OK. They didn't-- you know, the-- the Russians were there. The kids didn't understand much. They made, like, sports club. The younger people, everybody had to belong to sports. You have to walk, you have to make exercise. Everybody. There isn't a thing that you don't go. This was-- all the children have to go and belong to the sport, to teach them. As a matter of fact, I walk-- we walk-- walk to the city. Younger people, you walk 20 miles, back and forth. Going there, coming back. And I, myself, and another three girls, we came there first from the old city. We walked very fast. It helped me later, when I really had to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fact that your father could only work by himself, now, how did that make-- how did that change your life for you at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't so like it was before. You couldn't-- you couldn't be so-- you know, to make Shabbos or to sing like this. You do everything quiet. Because they were the Communists. They came in, and they want to change everything. They want to close the-- the synagogues. There were-- there was-- and the only thing they used to say, we have everything, we even have matches. They used to come and tell us that. Russia, they have everything. They have even matches, because the only thing there was matches. And they stayed in 1940, when they-- they start bombing. And people came running from the other side, from Hitler side. And they were telling, when Hitler comes, he-- he kills young boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not able to continue with your Jewish education and your Jewish activities, under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about September 1st, 1939? Were you aware of the war starting?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, the-- the Russians came in with tanks. They split it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I remember. The-- the people used to run out and see. That they came with big tanks and things. And all night, the Polish people were shooting, because nothing they could do about it. They took away everything, And leave us. We all were lying in the floor, because, you know, we're afraid we'll get killed. And that's all. The-- the-- the Communists took over, and they are the bosses. Nothing you can do about it. And then in-- when they start bombing, 1940, they start bombing. And I saw the bombs were coming straight at us, and they fall on the other side. I still now-- not now, but in the beginning-- when I came out from the war, when it was thundering, I thought that the bombs are coming. And then the-- the people telling that they kill younger boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people came from Hitler's side. They run away. And they were telling us that he kills young boys. Well, my brother was a younger boy. And my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother is that, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's in Israel now.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, which brother? Which one of the brothers were you making reference to when you say, my brother was a young boy. Are you talking about your youngest brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was the brother who was born in 1924. And my mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Label ?] Aryeh. And my mother said, take your brother and go to the-- to the border from there. Russian border. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was not [? tolerant about ?] a lot of kids. Younger kids, everybody was running away. And we were walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this because of the rumors that Hitler will kill the boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was how it was perceived in the community? That the thing to do was to run to the Russian border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian border. What could you do? The only thing to do was to run there, but we didn't know they were killing all the people, too. Maybe if people would know they killed all the people, they-- they could take wagons. They could do something. Because Russia let us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many children from your family ran away together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just myself and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother and you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had a little suitcase. Was so big. Because you have to walk. And I had a one dress, and we had change of underwear. That's all. Because if you have to walk, you have to be-- and I think we've got some food. Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 17. I wasn't even 17,

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers were one year younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else went with you from the neighboring families and friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw a lot of kids. A lot of kids walking and going. And-- you don't look at the other ones, you run and you don't know what. But then we were walking, and the-- [SIGHS] we had to go for 200 miles. First of all, they let us in by the border, right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no problem crossing the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the walk to the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a day and a night.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked through the whole day and the whole night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we come onto the-- it was Korets, and then it was the border. And they let us in. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- I know it was summer. It wasn't winter. So I don't remember the month. And going there, and a lot of kids were going. So the German airplanes used to fly on top, and come down and start shooting. So somebody was lucky. Came out-- we used to hide under the trees. The minute they saw the kids are going, they used to come down and ta, ta, ta, ta, killed everybody. We walked, and then some of the Russian machines-- you know, the-- the trucks-- they took us for 10 miles, and then they told us that the whole city was burning in Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne. We want to go back, because I have my-- my family and my parents. [SIGHS] And they wouldn't let us go back. They said, you just got back and you'll get killed, and you cannot go back. So we walked about 200 miles to Zhytomyr. And Zhytomyr, they let us-- they gave us a place where to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children would you say were in that exodus, that mass exodus. Of Rowne? Around you, roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of-- a lot of people, I can't even say. We were then 30 who-- who went together, but there was a lot-- maybe hundreds. I don't know what's happened to them. And they gave us tickets go to eat at the restaurant. The minute we walked out, we went to go eat, the planes came and they start bombing. So you couldn't go. Then, the Russian people, they took us to the train, to take us away farther from the war. It was a long train, just goes [INAUDIBLE]. The train were like cattle trains. And we were standing there, preparing, and the airplanes came, and they start throwing the bombs. In half of the train, the people got killed, because they bombed the trains. Luckily, I with my brother, we were on the other side and we came out alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other refugees on the train besides the children? Were you aware of other refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- one man was with us. He was the oldest, he was 30 years old. So he was like an old man. When you're young, and somebody's 30, it's like an old man. And this was, I mean-- the-- the only older man was. Otherwise, I didn't see. And we were on the train, they took us to Stalingrad. In Stalingrad, they put us in the horse-- in horse and carriages to a kolkhoz. A kolkhoz is a village that is a Communist. Everything goes together. Well, the whole thing was Communist, everything together. But this village we came there. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was outside of Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In there, they gave us, like-- some people took in the-- the people. Three. But these-- these families took three, this three-- the Russian families took in. And I, and my brother, and my girlfriend, they gave us a little shed that we stayed, like, private. And everybody had to go and work in the fields, because they took all the soldiers, they took away everybody. They need people to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this an organized thing from the government, or was this spontaneous Russians that wanted to help the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is everything from the government. They tell you to go there, that give you the horse and [?carriage?], and they take these people that-- Whatever they do is from the government. You don't do nothing by yourself. And then I told-- I knew how to sew, and I told-- there was a-- a-- the president from the-- from the thing and his wife, and I told her, I know how to sew. So she invited me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you say that to?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- they used to call him [? Presidatzer. ?] He was the president from the village. And I told his wife that I know how to sew dresses. So she invite me, and I was working by her. And every night, they didn't give me money, but they gave me milk, and they gave cheese. And I used to bring to my place, and all our friends used to come every evening and eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spent the day at her house, sewing for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: All day. But they were very nice people, I thought. They didn't know if we were Jewish or not Jewish. They didn't ask us. We didn't tell them. And we worked there-- we stayed there for a couple months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your brother and your friend that stayed with you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to go in the field and work. Everybody from there had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the exception.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only one, and then every woman invited me to come and to sew, because they didn't have anybody to do this. And-- and they-- you couldn't go out and buy anything. They had materials [? I never ?] wear, so I sew for them. Then we decided we cannot stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the war is coming, Hitler start going closer. And we said we had to run away from there because--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you aware of what was happening in the war? How did you-- where did you get information from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had radios. They had a radio, and they were talking. And the radio were talking. The people talked between them. And they say, it's coming. So we decided that we have to-- as a matter of fact, this man, he was like our leader. He said we have to go from here, to go and it's-- it's getting cold for winter. and we don't have clothes. We didn't have anything. And he said, we have to try to go to Stalingrad, back, and from there go to Asia. Because Asia is not so cold. So this lady, she was very nice, she baked me bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: What lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: The president's wife. She's the only one who knew that we are running away. She said, you go and I wouldn't tell anybody. And she-- she baked me bread, and she gave us food-- just for myself, and for my brother, but I split with the other people. Just going there, and at night--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you ran away together?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was 30 of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: All 30 ran away together?

speaker: interviewee
text: All 30, yes. We were all together. And the [INAUDIBLE] was-- we-- we had to slip in the middle of the night to walk, and we were-- just like they will come and they will get you. So we put fires around us. And we walked for a long way, and we came to Stalingrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked all the way from the kolkhoz to Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Take-- only take a couple days. We walked, we slept outside, [BACKROUND NOISE] and we walked, and we came to Stalingrad. And there was a lot of people, a lot of refugees. And there was wives. And-- the-- the officers' wives and children. They were running away from-- from the other cities. And it was like a football-- they used to play baseball or something in there. And there we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go to that football--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because from there, the-- the trains were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you know about the fact the trains were leaving from that football stadium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. And there we stayed a couple days. They then took us-- usually they used to take the wives and the children without-- they didn't make a big thing.
